reasoning: Mostly neutral paraphrase with some mild adoption of the article’s characterization (e.g., “hard-line anti-immigration agenda,” “far-right”) and a broadened evidence claim. Uses distancing terms like “allegedly” and “perceived,” but retains a few loaded descriptors from the source.

custom_reasoning.spin: Includes phrases like “anti-immigration stance” and “hard-line anti-immigration agenda,” and “closely mirrors far-right,” which add interpretive color.
custom_reasoning.unsubstantiated_claims: States “data and academic studies” show lower crime among immigrants; the article cites only one Stanford study.
custom_reasoning.opinion_as_fact: Characterizations such as “hard-line” and “anti-immigration agenda” are presented as settled descriptors.
custom_reasoning.sensationalism: Tone remains measured; no dramatic or alarmist language beyond source’s framing.
custom_reasoning.mind_reading: No claims about motives beyond what the cable states.
custom_reasoning.slant: Emphasizes critical framing (“despite data,” “far-right”) more than the administration’s stated rationale.
custom_reasoning.bias_by_omission: Omits nuance like support for high-skill immigration and the Afrikaner refugee detail, though space is limited.
custom_reasoning.word_choice: Politically loaded terms (“far-right,” “anti-immigration”) appear without attribution qualifiers.
custom_reasoning.negativity_bias: Highlights critical context and counterevidence more than supportive context.
custom_reasoning.elite_populist_bias: No clear deference to elites or grassroots; balanced in source types cited.
custom_reasoning.framing_neutrality: More neutral than source overall due to qualifiers, but retains some loaded framing.
custom_reasoning.perspective_completeness: Captures main perspectives (State Dept/administration, academic data) but mislabels a publisher as a perspective and misses some nuances.
